annotations.label: Gauge theory

anchor.snippet: A gauge theory is a type of theory in physics

annotations.note: Gauge theory is a physics framework with no formalization in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gauge transformation

anchor.snippet: is called a gauge transformation

annotations.note: Physical gauge transformations of fields are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gauge invariance

anchor.snippet: is a property called gauge invariance

annotations.note: Gauge invariance is a physics property with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Gauge symmetry

anchor.snippet: gauge invariance is sometimes called gauge symmetry

annotations.note: Gauge symmetry is a physics notion not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gauge theory (general criterion)

anchor.snippet: any theory that has the property of gauge invariance is considered a gauge theory

annotations.note: This characterization of gauge theories is physics, not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Observables vs potentials in electromagnetism

anchor.snippet: the electric field E and the magnetic field B are observable

annotations.note: Electromagnetic fields and potentials are not modeled in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Shifted potential solves Maxwell's equations

anchor.snippet: is also a solution to Maxwell's equations and no experiment can distinguish between these two solutions

annotations.note: Maxwell's equations and gauge freedom of their solutions are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gauge symmetry and energy conservation imply charge conservation

anchor.snippet: if gauge symmetry holds, and energy is conserved, then charge must be conserved

annotations.note: This Noether-type physics statement about charge conservation is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gauge transformations in general relativity

anchor.snippet: the gauge transformations for classical (i.e., non-quantum mechanical) general relativity are arbitrary coordinate transformations

annotations.note: General relativity and its coordinate gauge transformations are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Identical hydrogen atoms across space and time

anchor.section: An example of a symmetry in a physical theory: translation invariance
anchor.snippet: one observer examines the properties of a hydrogen atom on Earth

annotations.note: This physical thought experiment about hydrogen atoms has no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Translation invariance

anchor.section: An example of a symmetry in a physical theory: translation invariance
anchor.snippet: is called translation invariance

annotations.note: Translation invariance as a physical-theory symmetry is not formalized, though translation maps exist in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Arbitrary time-like coordinate redefinition

anchor.section: Another example of a symmetry: invariance of Einstein's field equation under arbitrary coordinate transformations
anchor.snippet: one can define an entirely new time-like coordinate according to some arbitrary rule

annotations.note: Relativistic coordinate redefinition is a physics example not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: General covariance

anchor.section: Another example of a symmetry: invariance of Einstein's field equation under arbitrary coordinate transformations
anchor.snippet: is customarily referred to as general covariance

annotations.note: General covariance of physical equations is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: General covariance is a special case of gauge invariance

anchor.section: Another example of a symmetry: invariance of Einstein's field equation under arbitrary coordinate transformations
anchor.snippet: General covariance is a special case of gauge invariance

annotations.note: This relationship between physics symmetries is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Maxwell's equations in generally covariant form

anchor.section: Another example of a symmetry: invariance of Einstein's field equation under arbitrary coordinate transformations
anchor.snippet: Maxwell's equations can also be expressed in a generally covariant form

annotations.note: Maxwell's equations in covariant form are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Double-slit experiment with electrons

anchor.snippet: if the double-slit experiment is performed with electrons, then a wave-like interference pattern is observed

annotations.note: The electron double-slit experiment is a physics phenomenon with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Electron wave frequency and kinetic energy relation

anchor.snippet: is related to the kinetic energy of an individual electron particle via the quantum-mechanical relation

annotations.note: This quantum-mechanical energy-frequency relation is physics, not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Aharonov–Bohm effect

anchor.snippet: One such example is the Aharonov–Bohm effect

annotations.note: The Aharonov–Bohm effect is a physics phenomenon not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Only phase difference affects the result

anchor.section: Gauge invariance: independence of results of gauge for the potentials
anchor.snippet: the only quantity that affects the result is the difference in phase between the two parts of the electron wave

annotations.note: This statement about quantum phase differences is physics, not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gauge symmetry needed for renormalizable QED

anchor.snippet: Gauge symmetry is required in order to make quantum electrodynamics a renormalizable theory

annotations.note: Renormalizability of QED is a quantum field theory result absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Electron phases form U(1)

anchor.snippet: electron phases form an Abelian group under addition, called the circle group or U(1)

annotations.note: The circle group U(1) is formalized as `Circle` with a `CommGroup` instance, exactly the abelian phase group described.

annotations.label: Abelian (commutativity)

anchor.snippet: means that addition is commutative

annotations.note: Commutativity of an abelian group is captured by `CommGroup` (via `mul_comm`/`add_comm`) in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Group (associativity, identity, inverse)

anchor.snippet: addition is associative , has an identity element

annotations.note: The `Group` class formalizes associativity, identity, and inverses exactly as described.

annotations.label: Other abelian groups

anchor.snippet: Other examples of abelian groups are the integers under addition

annotations.note: The integers under addition form an abelian group via `Int.instAddCommGroup` in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Twisting a cylinder as choice of gauge

anchor.snippet: consider whether it is possible to tell if a cylinder has been twisted

annotations.note: This intuitive analogy for gauge choice on a cylinder is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Noncommutative gauge groups and self-interacting fields

anchor.snippet: A noncommutative gauge group can describe a field that, unlike the electromagnetic field, interacts with itself

annotations.note: This statement about non-abelian gauge fields (Yang–Mills) is physics, not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Indistinguishable twins Alice and Betty

anchor.snippet: Imagine that Alice and Betty are identical twins

annotations.note: This illustrative analogy for indistinguishability has no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Momentum and wavelength relation

anchor.snippet: the momentum p of an individual particle is related to its wavelength λ by

annotations.note: The de Broglie momentum-wavelength relation is physics, not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gauge symmetry explains electromagnetic interactions

anchor.snippet: we have an explanation for the presence of electromagnetic interactions

annotations.note: Deriving electromagnetic interactions from gauge symmetry is physics, not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gauge bosons

anchor.snippet: such particles are called gauge bosons

annotations.note: Gauge bosons are physical particles with no formalization in Mathlib.
